title: Managing trickle vents after draughtproofing
subtitle: When to close, fit winter mode, or replace with a humidity-controlled alternative
excerpt: Trickle vents were fitted in most post-2002 UK double-glazed windows as Part F ventilation provision. Sealing them without providing an alternative creates condensation risk. This covers the decision framework, winter-mode operation, and when a humidity-controlled vent makes sense.
sourceNotes: Building Regulations Part F: Ventilation (2021 edition) for background ventilation requirements. Energy Saving Trust draught-proofing guidance. BRE report BR 443 on ventilation standards.

glossaryTerms.definition: Building Regulations Part F (Ventilation) sets minimum ventilation requirements for UK homes. It specifies background ventilation rates via trickle vents or equivalent.
A path through a building element with significantly higher heat conductivity than the surrounding material, creating a local cold spot and increased heat loss.

content.text: Trickle vents are the small slotted openings at the top of double-glazed window frames. They were required under
Part F

for windows installed in most new-build and replacement-window jobs from 2002 onwards. The standard requires a minimum equivalent area of 8,000 mm² per habitable room. Closing a trickle vent cuts that ventilation, and in a well-draughtproofed house can push a room below the minimum background ventilation rate.
What happens if you close them

In a house with good background ventilation from other sources (extract fans, natural infiltration), closing trickle vents rarely causes problems. In a well-sealed post-2000 house where a trickle vent is the main bedroom ventilation, closing it risks condensation in winter. A bedroom with two adults produces around 1 to 1.5 litres of water vapour per night through breathing.
Do not seal trickle vents in rooms with no other background ventilation source. The result is condensation on cold surfaces, mould growth, and potential damage to wall finishes. See the
ventilation after draughtproofing
guide for the full ventilation picture.
Winter-mode operation

Most trickle vents have a sliding closer on the inside of the frame. There is no harm in sliding it to reduce the open area in cold weather while the house is occupied and generating heat and moisture. The key is to leave at least a partial opening overnight in bedrooms: even a third of full opening is enough for background ventilation in a typical bedroom. Fully closed overnight is the condensation-risk position.
Humidity-controlled trickle vents

A humidity-controlled background ventilator (sold as a hygro-adjustable trickle vent) has a hygroscopic strip that opens the vent when humidity rises and closes it when the air is dry. The vent is self-regulating: open in summer, more closed in cold dry winters, and responsive to the rising humidity of an occupied bedroom overnight. Replacement units fit the same 35 mm slot as a standard trickle vent. Installation is four screws and takes around ten minutes per window.
Worked example: a 1990s semi

A 1995 semi-detached house in Leeds has replacement double glazing from 2004 with standard trickle vents. The owner has recently draughtproofed skirting boards and floorboard gaps. Total air permeability is now modest but not zero. The right approach: keep bedroom trickle vents on partial open overnight, close them during cold daytime periods when rooms are empty. The kitchen and bathroom already have intermittent extract fans as Part F requires. No replacement needed; behavioural management is sufficient.

If the owner fits MVHR (mechanical ventilation with heat recovery), trickle vents become redundant. MVHR provides continuous supply and extract at controlled rates. In that case, seal the trickle vents with a blanking plate from the vent manufacturer, not with foam or tape.
Common variations
Pre-2002 windows with no trickle vents: the building envelope was assumed to let in enough air. If you draughtproof these houses further, you need a clear plan for background ventilation.

Flats with a single-aspect window: one trickle vent may be the only background ventilation for the entire flat. Closing it in winter is riskier than in a house with multiple windows on different orientations.
Passive house standard retrofit: at this level of airtightness, trickle vents are sealed and all ventilation is mechanical. See the
ventilation after draughtproofing
guide.
Done well vs done badly
Done well: bedroom vents open partially overnight. Hygro-adjustable units in damp rooms. Blanked off where MVHR is installed.
Done badly: all trickle vents sealed with tape, no other background ventilation, condensation appearing on window reveals and cold wall corners within one winter. A
thermal bridge
at a window reveal with no ventilation is the classic mould site.
